text: <par></par>

<par>How to distinguish <reconstruction>*s-ku</reconstruction> from <reconstruction>*guŋ</reconstruction>. Cf. Dulong Nujiang <latinform>gɔŋ¹³</latinform> BODY, <latinform>ʃi⁵⁵gɯ⁵⁵</latinform> CORPSE. Dulong has <latinform>aŋ³¹</latinform> prefix like Bisu/Sangkong/Phunoi. Akha, Burmish forms w/ defunct tag 1803 <latinform>raŋ⪤waŋ</latinform>:Akha: <latinform>g'aw</latinform><latinform>ˇmawˇ BODY,</latinform> Bola <latinform>ɣɔ⁵⁵</latinform><latinform>kɔ̃⁵⁵</latinform> CHEST, Lashi <latinform>jaŋ³¹</latinform><latinform>kaŋ³¹</latinform> CHEST, Zaiwa <latinform>vaŋ⁵¹</latinform><latinform>-kaŋ⁵¹</latinform> CHEST, Hani (Dazai) <latinform>ɣo⁵⁵</latinform><latinform>mo⁵⁵</latinform> BODY, Hani (Shuikui) <latinform>ʒu³³</latinform><latinform>ɣu⁵⁵</latinform> BODY, Hani (Lüchun) <latinform>jò vó</latinform> BODY, Langsu <latinform>vɔ̃³¹</latinform><latinform>(kɔ̃³¹)</latinform> CHEST, Sani <latinform>ɣo̱²¹</latinform> BODY (why constricted?).</par>
<par></par>
<par>Naga copulas in lei (GEM, WTF), etc. may go with Kiranit *let. Add LTSR 68.</par>
<par>In the semantic flowchart, the connection BREATH(E)-HEART is found in Karenic.</par>
<par>check WT <latinform>mnyal</latinform> 'lie down to sleep.'</par>
<par>re-process SWELL (3/11/93). Allofamize e163.</par>
<par>add drawing: HEAD — NECK.</par>

<par>General-tendency semantics: <reconstruction>*mul</reconstruction> refers to fine body-hair; <reconstruction>*tsam</reconstruction> coarse body-hair like pubic, armpit, etc; <reconstruction>*ney</reconstruction> head hair; <reconstruction>*s-kra</reconstruction> head-hair, head; <reconstruction>*s-pwa</reconstruction> single hair. <reconstruction>*sâm</reconstruction> HAIR, TR <latinform>sem</latinform> ‘head hair.’ </par>

<par>A great number of verbs are usable with HEAD to convey the meaning of BALD, e.g. WB <latinform>khôŋ proŋ</latinform> ‘head + clean/clear’ ; WB <latinform>thip proŋ</latinform> ‘top + clean/clear’ ; <latinform>khôŋ tûm</latinform> ‘head + dull/blunt’ ; <latinform>khôŋ tûm-tui</latinform> ‘head + blunt-short’ ; <latinform>û phrâñ</latinform> ‘head + slow (?).’ </par>
<par>drawing of <gloss>brain/fat/marrow</gloss> < Mikir?</par>
<par>where are ety 1116-1122??</par>
<par>Re: the WING -- GILL -- EYEBROW connexion, cf. Eng 'flutter'. Also e2077 Mag., AM, WT <latinform>mig-gs̀og </latinform> 'eyelash'.</par>
<par>GSR 532c <latinform>li̯wəd/lywi </latinform>really <latinform>< *kləy</latinform> 'shit?' STC calls <latinform>śi̯ər/śi </latinform>cognate to <reconstruction>*kləy</reconstruction>.</par>
<par><reconstruction>*guːŋ</reconstruction>. WTF 527. But cf. Lh. <latinform>nèmdash a-qhɔ̂-qhɔ</latinform>.</par>

<par>Note that in the Garo form for ‘waist’ ko-mor, the second syllable is apparently from etymon 467 *s-m<latinform>ːr MOUTH. The semantics are peculiar, and this etymology may need to be reconsidered. See the footnote under the Garo form and the note at Chapter 4.6 WAIST.</latinform> </par><par>  In the drawing for MOUTH labeled "I-E", the "k" of CHEEK is being obliterated by the semicircle figure. Maybe this will be fixed automatically in newer Superpaint, or by pasting into Word. </par><par>  Nepali for SNOUT? Chinese <latinform>chún ↭ zǔi</latinform>.</par>

<par>STC recognizes only one root for BEAK/BILL, <reconstruction>*muːr</reconstruction> (#366), which is also glossed MOUTH/FACE/GILLS. Many TB lgs simply have no special words for this meaning, and the words for ‘mouth’ or ‘lip’ are used instead. Many languages employ compound patterns like BIRD-MOUTH, BIRD-LIP, BIRD-TOOTH, HORN-LIP etc.</par>

<par><xref ref="452">#452</xref> *bur or ber [<latinform>bər</latinform>] should be corrected to *bul, on the basis of the form in Milang, a coda-conservative Mirish lg that distinguishes -r and *-l.</par>
<par>Many lgs manifests a <latinform>-č(ʰ)i</latinform> form (Tamang, Tsangla, Kokborok, Bokar etc.). A new root? Or reflexes of <reconstruction>*(t)siŋ</reconstruction>?</par>
<par><xref ref="637">#637</xref> <reconstruction>*kəŋ</reconstruction> may need to be modified to <reconstruction>*kwəŋ</reconstruction> in view of the Jingpho form.</par>
<par>change <xref ref="637">#637</xref> gloss to TOOTH/TUSK</par>
<par>ST lgs usually have the structure DOG+TOOTH, e.g. Chinese, Taraon, Kaman, etc.</par>

<par>Many TB words meaning MOLAR are compounds: Lepcha <latinform>fo-góm</latinform> and Bokar <latinform>jiː-kam</latinform> are compounds of TOOTH + JAW. (See <xref ref="630">#630</xref>.) Lahu <latinform>cì-g̈àʔ-ma</latinform> 'molar' is a compound of <xref ref="635">#635</xref> and <xref ref="5669">#5669</xref>. Lushai (Mizo) <latinform>ha-pui</latinform> 'molar' is a compound of <xref ref="632">#632</xref> and <xref ref="1622">#1622</xref>.</par>

<par>Most TB languages reflect the PTB root <reconstruction>*r-nil ⪤ s-nil</reconstruction>, which STC claims to be possibly related to PTB<reconstruction>*r-ni </reconstruction>‘red’ . The Northern Qiang forms for ‘gums’ <latinform>-zdi</latinform> and ‘red’ <latinform>ɕi-</latinform> (Taoping <latinform>χȵi-</latinform>), however, seem to come from different initials. The form <latinform>khV-</latinform> is also common in BL and Qiangic (Bradley reconstructs Proto-Loloish <reconstruction>*ʔ-ko³</reconstruction> on the basis of Lahu Shi and Southern Loloish). Attested compound formations in TB include TOOTH + GUMS, TOOTH + RED, TOOTH + BASE and TOOTH + FOOT; the Chinese formation TOOTH + FLESH is also attested.</par>
<par><xref ref="1147">#1147</xref>: for the variation between -t and -p within a single lg see the Bahing forms 142741 and 121862.</par>

<par>PAF of <xref ref="688">#688</xref> <reconstruction>*wa-t</reconstruction>, <xref ref="1761">#1761</xref> <reconstruction>*ŋr/yat</reconstruction>, <xref ref="1762">#1762</xref> <latinform>N-gwya</latinform> is <latinform>N/s g-w-y-a-t</latinform> cf. LTBA 9.1.</par>
<par><xref ref="486">#486</xref> add WINDPIPE/NECK/GULLET to gloss, cf. WT lkog-ma ‘windpipe, gullet, neck, throat’ .</par>
<par><xref ref="499">#499</xref> attested in Achang and Yakkha.</par>
<par><xref ref="499">#499</xref>: add WINDPIPE to gloss, cf. Lepcha form for ‘windpipe’ .</par>
<par>change <xref ref="486">#486</xref> to <reconstruction>*C-kok</reconstruction>.</par>

<par>Note the semantic connection between LARYNX and KNOT or SEED., e.g. WT <latinform>ʼol-mdud</latinform> ‘throat-knot’ ; WB <latinform>lañ-ceʼ</latinform> ‘neck-seed’ ; Lisu <latinform>tɕeɑ³⁵miʔ²¹tɔ⁵⁵si'̀a a¹</latinform> ‘monkey-knot’. </par><par>Note the similarity between the Lolopho form <latinform>ɒ⁴⁴ kɤ̱⁴⁴ lɤ̱⁴⁴</latinform>, the Lahu form <latinform>qá-qə̂ʔ-lə̂ʔ</latinform>, and the Lisu form <latinform>bø⁴⁴ kɔ²¹ lɔ³⁵</latinform> (all 'larynx/Adam's apple'). There seems to be a Loloish binome here, currently tagged <xref ref="486">#486</xref> + <xref ref="3412">#3412</xref>, but this could just be imitative, as in English 'gullet', 'glug'.</par>

<par>Note the following three WT forms for ‘throat’: <latinform>ʼog-ma  ⪤ lkog-ma ↮ ʼol-ma</latinform>. </par><par>  It is difficult to distinguish among the velar-initialed etyma: <xref ref="486">#486</xref>, <xref ref="495">#495</xref>, and <xref ref="3361">#3361</xref>. Compare Sangkong <latinform>aŋ³³khoŋ³¹</latinform> and Phunoi <latinform>ʔã⁵⁵ko¹¹</latinform> (both 'throat'). The Phunoi form cannot be from <reconstruction>*-waŋ</reconstruction>, since <reconstruction>*-waŋ > on/õ</reconstruction>, cf. ‘wing’ <latinform>ʔã́ tṍ</latinform> < <xref ref="711">#711</xref>, ‘thigh’ <latinform>ʔã́-pon</latinform> < <xref ref="404">#404</xref>.</par>

<par>Note the semantic connection between NECK and WAIST; the latter is the neck of the body. This is analogous to the extension of words for 'neck' to other thin flexible areas of the body, as in Korean <latinform>son-mok</latinform> 'wrist' ('hand neck') and <latinform>pal-mok</latinform> 'ankle' ('foot neck'). There is thus probably a relationship between WT <latinform>sked-pa ~ rked-pa</latinform> 'waist; middle' and <latinform>ske ~ skye ~ skya</latinform> 'neck, throat'.</par><par>  For the semantic connection between NECK and SHOULDER, see Chapter 1.4.3. </par><par>  The Karen forms, which all look something like <latinform>ko</latinform>, could either be from <xref ref="486">#486</xref> or from <xref ref="3361">#3361</xref>.</par><par>  Note also the similarity between the Hani forms for ‘neck’ <latinform>kʰɔ³¹ lɔ⁵⁵</latinform>, <latinform>kʰɔ³¹ laŋ⁵⁵</latinform>, <latinform>kʰɯ³¹ l̥ɯ⁵⁵</latinform> and the Karen (B) form <latinform>kʰo³¹ lø³¹ tʰi⁵⁵</latinform>. </par><par>  There is a widespread binome in LB consisting of etyma <xref ref="479">#479</xref> + <xref ref="255">#255</xref>.</par><par>  There is a possible semantic connection between THROAT/NECK and HEART; the first syllables of Limbu <latinform>niŋ-mā</latinform> ‘throat’ and Bisu <latinform>nɨŋ-tshɨ</latinform> ‘neck’ mean ‘heart’ .</par>
<par></par>

<par>For the semantic relationship with KNEE, see Chapter 1.5.8.4 (<xref ref="436">#436</xref>).</par><par>  Note also the semantic connection between SHOULDER and the adjoining NECK. <xref ref="563">#563</xref> is probably related to <xref ref="479">#479</xref>.</par><par>  Some words for SHOULDER are derived from roots meaning CONVEX, as WB <latinform>paʼ khûm</latinform>. </par><par>  Note the general proliferation of etyma for SHOULDER  with labial initials.</par><par>  Note the striking similarity between the Lahu form <latinform>làʔ-qá-pɨ</latinform> and Qiang (Taoping) <latinform>lɑ³¹ χɑ⁵⁵ pi³³</latinform>, both meaning ‘shoulder’ and both tagged as <xref ref="695">#695</xref> + <xref ref="1278">#1278</xref> + <xref ref="276">#276</xref>.</par>

<par>Note the relationship between CHEST and BELLY; the Kiranti root <xref ref="3378">#3378</xref> is from <xref ref="137">#137</xref>.</par><par><xref ref="2">#2</xref> is the source for WT <latinform>sku-braŋ</latinform> ‘chest (resp.)’, and also of the honorific prefix used with many body parts in WT. It should be kept distinct from <xref ref="560">#560</xref>, which is reflected in Thakali <latinform>'ku</latinform>, Kaike <latinform>gu</latinform>, etc. </par><par>It is interesting to note that both ‘chest’ and ‘trunk’ are ambiguous in English between body-part and receptacle!</par>

For the formations WAIST + BONE = RIB and BELLY + BONE = RIB, see Chapters 1.4.6 and 1.4.7.
The two roots #553 and #297 are sometimes difficult to distinguish, especially in Lolo-Burmese, but in general it seems that #553 is more typical of Burmish, while #297 is more typical of Loloish.

<par>The Angami, Jirel, Kulung, Moyon, Bodo, Lalung, and Apatani forms appear to be closely related.</par><par>  Note the relation WAIST + BONE = RIB in Phunoi <latinform>ʔã cò</latinform> ‘waist’, <latinform>ʔɑ̃⁵⁵ co¹¹ jɑu¹¹</latinform> ‘rib’ (<latinform>jɑu¹¹</latinform> is ‘bone’); for the similar construction BELLY + BONE = RIB, see Chapter 1.4.7. </par><par>  Note that in the Loloish languages (e.g. Lisu, Xide, Gazhuo), compounds for WAIST often have <xref ref="418">#418</xref> as a constituent; these morphemes should not be confused with <xref ref="223">#223</xref>. </par><par>  Note the following NE Indian ‘parallel’ binomes which appear to have the same etymological structure, all glossed ‘waist’: Meluri <latinform>a-khü-shi</latinform>; Miji <latinform>gɯ-tsiʔ</latinform>; Miri <latinform>ki-čik</latinform>; Galo <latinform>pak-pu-cik</latinform> (last syllable only); Ntenyi <latinform>a-khü-shü</latinform>. The final syllables may be stop-final allofams of <xref ref="223">#223</xref>; the first syllables may possibly be related to <xref ref="211">#211</xref> or <xref ref="217">#217</xref>.</par>

<par>Note the relation BELLY + BONE = RIB in Apatani <latinform>a-kʰrjɯ</latinform> ‘belly’ , <latinform>a-kʰrjɯ a-lo</latinform> ‘rib’ (<latinform>a-lo</latinform> is ‘bone’ ); for the similar construction WAIST + BONE = RIB, see Chapter 1.4.6.</par>

<par>Many Lolo-Burmese forms for 'back' are apparently descended from a bi-syllabic protoform <xref ref="225">#225</xref> + <xref ref="219">#219</xref>, as Bisu <latinform>taŋ-khaŋ</latinform>. We also note the frequent pattern <xref ref="1">#1</xref> + <xref ref="225">#225</xref>, as Lisu <latinform>gu³⁵ du³¹</latinform>. Lolo-Burmese languages show no real evidence for <xref ref="211">#211</xref> or <xref ref="206">#206</xref>. </par><par>  Note the following forms for ‘back’ : Sde-dge Tibetan <latinform>dʑɑʔ³¹loʔ⁵³</latinform>, Sherpa <latinform>'gyap-lā</latinform>, Shixing <latinform>dʑo³³lɑ³⁵</latinform>. The second syllables seem to be related to WT <latinform>logs</latinform> ‘side’, for which we have no PTB etymology.</par>

<par>Note the collocation of BUTT and HEAVY for words meaning 'lazy; sluggish'. See Jingpho <latinform>maidang li</latinform> 'heavy, slow, lazy'; Lahu <latinform>qhɛ̂-qhɔ hɔ̂ </latinform>'sluggish, slow-moving; lazy'. (Note also the English expression 'lead in your pants', and Japanese <latinform>koshi ga omoi</latinform> 'hips are heavy'.) </par><par>  It is interesting to note that Padam-Mising [Abor-Miri] preserves four distinct PTB roots in four synonyms glossed 'buttock': <latinform>ko-dun</latinform>, <latinform>ko-piang</latinform>, <latinform>ta-pong</latinform>, <latinform>ki-pum</latinform> (roots <xref ref="574">#574</xref>, <xref ref="399">#399</xref>, <xref ref="404">#404</xref>, and <xref ref="163">#163</xref> resp.).</par>

<par>For the first attempt to deal with this area of semantic space, see Matisoff 1985 ('Out on a limb').</par><par>The presentation of the roots follows a certain roughly logical order. First come the etyma that seem basically to have meant HAND. The most widespread of these, which we might call «the <reconstruction>*lak</reconstruction>-family», comes in some nine variant forms (allofams), with semantic spillover into the areas of CUBIT and FOOT (Etyma #'s 1a-1i). This HAND section also comprises 6 more etyma, of varying degrees of solidity (#'s 2-7).</par><par>Next come the roots whose basic meaning is deemed to have been ARM (#'s 8-15), followed by the more numerous etyma in the WING area (#'s 17-32), one of which (#17) has 5 variants. This study is rounded out by several roots meaning PALM or WRIST (#'s 33-37); then three roots referring to measurements made with the upper limb (CUBIT, HANDSPAN; #'s 38-40); finally an etymon with both nominal (ARMPIT) and verbal (TICKLE) dimensions (#41), and a group of 7 Proto-Kuki-Chin reconstructions referring to actions performed with the hands (#'s 42-48).</par><par>Most of the reconstructions offered are at the Proto-Tibeto-Burman level. When available, meso-reconstructions are also included (e.g. pKaren, pKiranti, pKuki-Chin, pLolo-Burmese, pTani). Usually these are subsumed under the PTB etymon they represent, and do not receive a separate number (#'s 1c, 2, 8, 19, 20b, 22), but occasionally they have so far only been reconstructed for a single subgroup, and so receive separate numbers (#'s 15, 26, 30, 42-48).</par><par>	For 13 of these etyma, Chinese comparanda are suggested, so that they may plausibly be reconstructed back to the Proto-Sino-Tibetan level.</par><par>	For the sake of completeness we include an Indo-Aryan root (#32) which has been borrowed into several Himalayan languages.</par><par>	Detailed as these etymologies are, they by no means exhaust the semantic field of the UPPER LIMB, which includes such notions as FINGER, FIST, ELBOW, NAIL, etc.</par>

<par>Note the connection between HAND and LEAF, cf. Nocte <latinform>dʌk</latinform> ‘hand’ , <latinform>¹ʌ-dʌk</latinform> ‘leaf’ ; Konyak (Tamlu) <latinform>yak</latinform> ‘hand’ , <latinform>bɯn-yak</latinform> ‘leaf’ ; Garo <latinform>jak</latinform> ‘hand’ ; <latinform>bi-jak</latinform> ‘leaf’ ; etc. In other languages, the words for ‘hand’ and ‘foot’ both contain the same morpheme, which is allofamically related to a morpheme in ‘leaf’ . For example, Lisu <latinform>lá⁶-hpá²</latinform> ‘hand’ , <latinform>hchi³-hpá²</latinform> ‘foot’ , <latinform>si²-hpyá⁵</latinform> ‘leaf’.</par>
<par>Note that words for 'heel' are sometimes collocations of FOOT + EGG or FOOT + BUTTOCK.</par>
<par>There are also compounds HAND-MOTHER.</par>
<par>For etyma meaning LEG, see Chapter 1.5.1.</par>
<par>cf. WRIST, KNUCKLE (ELBOW, KNEE). chart?</par>

<par>Note the semantic connection between SHOULDER (Chapter 1.4.3) and KNEE; cf. Nung <latinform>phut</latinform> ‘shoulder’ and Jingpho <latinform>ləphùt</latinform> ‘knee’ (< <xref ref="436">#436</xref>).</par>
<par>Note that etymon 350 *la LEG is homophonous to etymon 701 *la ARM.</par>

<par>Note that quite a number of Himalayish languages have borrowed Nepali <latinform>hāD</latinform> (<latinform>hāḍ</latinform>) 'bone' for words for 'bone' and 'rib'. See Turner p. 807, who reconstructs <reconstruction>*haḍḍa-</reconstruction> (Ass. <latinform>hār</latinform>; Beng. <latinform>hāṛ</latinform>) and notes that the "connection with <latinform>aṣṭhi</latinform> very doubtful".</par>

<par>The fact that so many roots can be reconstructed for SKIN is undoubtedly due to the wide variety of associations this concept has with other points in semantic space: human, animal, and vegetable. The notion of <emph>outer covering</emph> is equally applicable to the skin of humans, the hide of animals, the bark of trees, the rind of fruits, the husks of corn, or the shells of nuts.<footnote>Macadamia nuts have both husks and shells.</footnote> Since several sub-parts of the body have their own skin-like coverings, morphemes for SKIN appear in many compound words for such specialized body-parts as eyelids, lips, and foreskins. A further semantic chain leads from the body-part to the associated verb, TO SKIN/REMOVE THE SKIN.  Semantic flowcharts provide a visual representation of SKIN and related concepts; the associations with SKIN are in fact so complex that two flowcharts are given.</par><par>Etyma in this semantic area are reconstructed with widely different degrees of certitude, ranging from the rock-solid to the shaky.  They are presented below roughly in the order of the strength of their attestations.</par>
<par>For the possible semantic connection between HEART and NECK, see Chapter 1.4.2.</par>
<par>The flowchart below depicts the semantic relationship between LUNGS and LIVER in Jingpho.</par>

<par>As expected, some TB forms for this gloss are onomatopoeic (or onomatopoeic form plus a generic verb, cf. Southern Min <latinform>pʰa³¹ kʰa³³tɕʰĩũ²¹</latinform>). Such forms do not seem analyzable into individual morphemes</par>
<par>evidence of the<latinform>-s </latinform>for the reconstruction<reconstruction>*(m-)pras</reconstruction>: rGyalrong (NW), Tsangla, WT, Chepang, Kanauri, Kulung.</par>

<par>New tagging number: 1145 <reconstruction>*m-pras</reconstruction>. Evidence of nasal prefix: rGyalrong <latinform>m</latinform><latinform>pʰɐt</latinform>; Jingpho <latinform>n</latinform><latinform>-phat</latinform>; Kaman <latinform>tʌ-</latinform><latinform>m</latinform><latinform>phat</latinform>. Evidence of <latinform>-s</latinform> final: Tsangla <latinform>pʰro</latinform><latinform>s</latinform>, Kanauri <latinform>pha</latinform><latinform>s</latinform><latinform>-</latinform>; Chepang <latinform>hèmdash a</latinform><latinform>s</latinform>; Thulung <latinform>pho</latinform><latinform>s</latinform>; Bantawa <latinform>pe</latinform><latinform>s</latinform>; rGyalrong (NW) <latinform>kə-mbe</latinform><latinform>s</latinform>; WT <latinform>'phro</latinform><latinform>s</latinform> 'to come out'. Evidence of <latinform>-r-</latinform> medial: Tsangla <latinform>pʰ</latinform><latinform>r</latinform><latinform>os</latinform>, WT <latinform>'ph</latinform><latinform>r</latinform><latinform>os</latinform> 'to come out', Nusu <latinform>pʰ</latinform><latinform>ɹ</latinform><latinform>ə̱³¹</latinform>; Pattani <latinform>p</latinform><latinform>r</latinform><latinform>ãsi;</latinform> Mao <latinform>¹p</latinform><latinform>r</latinform><latinform>a</latinform>; Khezha <latinform>²te²p</latinform><latinform>r</latinform><latinform>e</latinform>; Nyisu (Mirish) <latinform>b</latinform><latinform>l</latinform><latinform>â.</latinform></par>

<par><xref ref="650">#650</xref> <latinform>tši</latinform> should be modified as <reconstruction>*tši~dži</reconstruction>; cf. Lahu Na <latinform>dzɿ⁵³</latinform>; Jingpho <latinform>džit</latinform>.</par>

<par>Most Qiangish lgs (and Naxi) have reflexes with a voiced (often prenasalized or preceded by some other consonantal element) bilabial stop and an open rhyme with a front vowel (cf. Mawo Qiang <latinform>/bi/</latinform>). This seems to warrant postulating a spesific proto-Qiangish root <xref ref="1102">#1102</xref> <reconstruction>*C-bi</reconstruction>.</par>
<par>Forms in some Bodic lgs seem to be nursery words. ex. Tibetan <latinform>ka-ka</latinform>; Bunan <latinform>ko-ko</latinform>, Spiti <latinform>a-ka</latinform>.</par>
<par>Many lgs have the pattern FART+let go to denote ‘to break wind’ , e.g. Chinese, Tibetan, Loloish.</par>
<par>Is it possible to collapse 1293 *s-bu and 1295 *s-pa (how is the vowel arrived at?).</par>
<par>New tagging numbers: <xref ref="1103">#1103</xref> <reconstruction>*krap</reconstruction> (STC #116); <xref ref="1104">#1104</xref> <reconstruction>*ŋuw</reconstruction> (STC#79).</par>
<par>Common compound patterns are : EYE+water, EYE+TEARS.</par>
<par>Most of the etyma in this chapter were previously published in <emph>The Tibeto-Burman Reproductive System</emph> (Matisoff 2008).</par>

<par>The English word <emph>yolk</emph> (< OE <emph>geolca</emph>) is an allofam of <emph>yellow</emph> (< OE <emph>geolu</emph>) < PIE <reconstruction>*ghel-</reconstruction> 'shine; shiny object' [AHD:2029]. Similarly, TB words for YOLK typically contain a morpheme meaning YELLOW, e.g. Lahu <latinform>g̈âʔ-u-ši</latinform> 'yolk of hen's egg' < <xref ref="1606">#1606</xref> + <xref ref="301">#301</xref> + <latinform>ši</latinform> 'yellow/gold' < <xref ref="3496">#3496</xref>. See HPTB:191.</par>

<par>An important associated concept is BIRD, with several distinct roots appearing in compounds for EGG, including <xref ref="1603">#1603</xref>, <xref ref="1600">#1600</xref>, <xref ref="2187">#2187</xref>, <xref ref="1604">#1604</xref>, <xref ref="3442">#3442</xref>. It is sometimes hard to distinguish reflexes of <reconstruction>*wa</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*haːr</reconstruction>; cf. the first syllable in Wancho <latinform>ao-ti</latinform>, below. Similarly, it is sometimes hard to distinguish reflexes of <reconstruction>*wa</reconstruction> and <reconstruction>*bya</reconstruction>; cf. the first syllable in Bahing <latinform>ʔba-di</latinform>.</par>
<par>The roots in this chapter refer primarily to the female breast as part of the reproductive system, rather than BREAST in the sense of CHEST (but see <xref ref="3463">#3463</xref>, below).</par>

<par>Etyma for VAGINA and PENIS show frequent interchange and/or phonological similarity, by a phenomenon felicitously dubbed "genital flipflop" by Benedict.<footnote>Benedict 1979, "A note on Karen genital flipflop" (LTBA 5.1:21-24); 1981 "A further (unexpurgated) note on Karen genital flipflop" (LTBA 6.1:103); 1991 "Genital flipflop: a Chinese note" (LTBA 14.1:143-6).</footnote> The roots where this interchange is most evident include: <xref ref="661">#661</xref> : <xref ref="3420">#3420</xref>; <xref ref="1616">#1616</xref> : <xref ref="1284">#1284</xref>; <xref ref="1352">#1352</xref> : <xref ref="641">#641</xref>; <xref ref="1353">#1353</xref> : <xref ref="547">#547 PENIS / MALE / SON-IN-LAW</xref>. This relationship is indicated by a <emph>yin-yang</emph> curve in the semantic diagram.</par>

<par>Roots for VAGINA also show interchange with etyma for MOUTH. See especially <xref ref="1356">#1356</xref>; <xref ref="686">#686</xref>; and <xref ref="667">#667</xref>.</par><par>There is also an association between VAGINA and ANUS (see, e.g. <xref ref="3432">#3432</xref> and <xref ref="665">#665</xref>).</par>
<par>An association between CLITORIS and UVULA is shown by Mizo <latinform>daŋ-mɔn</latinform> 'uvula', literally "palate-clitoris". See VSTB, p. 67.</par>

<par>We are not including roots for FEMALE/WOMAN in this chapter, since they seem quite independent of etyma for the "female organs". (Apparent exceptions are a few forms from rGyalrong, e.g. NW rGyalrong <latinform>tə◦stə</latinform> 'female' < <xref ref="661">#661</xref>; but these may be misglossed.) On the other hand, words for MALE do frequently interchange with words for the male genitals; some of these etyma are accordingly discussed in Chapter 1.9.7 below. </par>
<par>Note that common terms for womb are “child-house” ,” child-nest” , and possibly “child-aid” .</par>

<par>Words for WOMB or PLACENTA are frequently associated with notions of NEST, HOUSE, or BELLY. A slightly different metaphor is exemplified by Kanauri <latinform>chaŋ khŭl</latinform>, lit. "child skin", with the second element < <xref ref="593">#593</xref>.</par>

<par>The various components of the male genitalia show semantic associations with a number of other bodyparts, as well as with a wide range of objects in other semantic fields. Some of these associations are widespread or universal in the world's languages; others seem more idiosyncratic to ST or the SEA'n linguistic area.</par><par>Among the universal semantic associations we may list the following:</par><par>(1) PENIS <--> TAIL: The English word <emph>penis</emph> itself is from Lat. <emph>pēnis</emph> 'tail' (cf. also Fr. <emph>queue</emph> < Lat. <emph>cauda</emph> 'tail'), among countless other examples. The widespread TB etymon for TAIL <xref ref="1288">#1288 </xref> <reconstruction>*may ⪤ *mey ⪤ *mi</reconstruction> (cf. also Chinese <hanform>尾</hanform> OC <reconstruction>*mi̯wər</reconstruction>) has similar associations, e.g. Jingpho <latinform>mài</latinform>, <latinform>ǹ-mài</latinform> 'tail; a euphemistic name for the male private parts' (Hanson 1906/1954, p. 400).</par><par>(2) PENIS <--> BANANA: This connection is made explicitly in Kokborok <latinform>tʰa-li</latinform> (see <xref ref="1284">#1284</xref> below) and in Newar <latinform>mwā-e</latinform> 'banana; penis'.</par><par>(3) GLANS PENIS <--> PENIS + HEAD: This is a universal association (cf. WT <latinform>mje-mgo</latinform>; Lahu <latinform>nī-ó-qō</latinform>; Lisu (Northern) <latinform>ɔ⁵⁵dy³³</latinform> 'head', <latinform>hɔ̃²¹ɔ⁵⁵dy³³</latinform> 'penis'; Yakha <latinform>tu◦khəruk</latinform> 'head', <latinform>liː◦goː u◦tu◦kəruk</latinform> 'glans'; Qiang (Mawo) <latinform>qə̥ pætʂ</latinform> 'head', <latinform>li◦qə̥ pætʂ</latinform> 'glans'; Eng. <emph>dick-head</emph>).</par><par>(4) TESTICLE <--> EGG: This is also a universal association (cf. e.g. Russian <emph>jajtsy</emph> 'eggs; testicles', Hebrew <emph>beytsim</emph> 'id.'). The same etymon may have some reflexes meaning EGG and others meaning TESTICLE. Several TB etyma showing this association have been treated above under EGG (Chapter 1.9.1); others will be treated below in this chapter. Alternatively a language may have a compound word for TESTICLE of the structure PENIS + EGG.</par><par>(5) TESTICLE <--> FRUIT / BALL / ROUNDED OBJECT: TB compounds for TESTICLE frequently take the form PENIS + FRUIT/ROUND OBJECT, e.g. Lahu <latinform>nī-šī</latinform> 'testicle' (2nd syll. < <xref ref="1019">#1019</xref>).<footnote>Note that TESTICLE <--> NUT is not characteristic of SEA, since languages in this region generally lack a word for NUT distinct from FRUIT. Cf. Chinese <hanform>果</hanform> (Mand. <latinform>guǒ</latinform>), used equally well for FRUIT (more specifically <hanform>水果</hanform> Mand. <latinform>shuǐguǒ</latinform> "water-fruit") and NUT, e.g. <hanform>開心果</hanform> Mand. <latinform>kāixīnguǒ</latinform> 'pistachio' (lit. "open-heart-fruit").</footnote></par><par>Cf. also Newar <latinform>kwaa-si</latinform> 'testicles' (lit. "hot-fruit"), reminiscent of the vulgar English simile "hot as balls".</par><par>(6) TESTICLE <--> STONE: Also a widespread association in the world's languages, including archaic English slang. In TB, cf. formations like Monpa <latinform>khong-lung</latinform> 'testicle' (<latinform>lung</latinform> 'stone').</par><par>(7) SCROTUM <--> BASKET / BAG / POUCH / NEST: This obvious association occurs in Lat. <emph>scrōtum</emph> < <emph>scrautum</emph> 'leather pouch for arrows', and is also found in TB, e.g. Lahu <latinform>nī-sī-phɨ</latinform> 'scrotum', <latinform>phɨ</latinform> 'nest; receptacle' < <xref ref="1611">#1611</xref>. Cf. also <xref ref="3471">#3471</xref>, <xref ref="643">#643</xref>.</par><par>(8) SEMEN <--> PENIS + LIQUID: Cf. Nesu <latinform>le̱ṟ³³ ʑʅ²¹</latinform> and Noesu <latinform>ɬu⁵⁵ʑi²¹</latinform>, where the second element < <xref ref="1013">#1013</xref>, and WB <latinform>lî-rañ</latinform>.</par>

Semantic associations that seem more peculiar to the SEA'n linguistic area include the following:(9) PENIS <--> VAGINA: Many etyma have some reflexes which refer to the male organ, but others which refer to the female counterpart, a phenomenon that has been dubbed 'genital flipflop' (see chapter note under VAGINA). Etyma like this should perhaps be assigned the gender-neutral gloss GENITALS at the proto-level. Several have been treated under VAGINA (Chapter 1.9.5, above); others will be treated below in this chapter.(10) PENIS <--> BODY + TONGUE: Cf. Garo maŋ-sre 'penis' < #17 + #623 *s-lya TONGUE. This form was cited in Benedict 1979:24.(11) TESTICLE <--> SEED: Cf. WB lî-ce’, lit. "penis-seed".(12) GLANS <--> NOTCH: Cf. WB lî-thac 'corona glandis', lit. "penis-notch", and Lahu nī-qhɛ̀ʔ 'penis', where the second syllable means 'notch (as a stick); chip; break off a piece'.(13) GLANS <--> BUD: Cf. WB lî-ŋum 'glans', lit. "penis-bud".(14) GLANS <--> TURTLE: While Latin glāns means 'acorn', Mandarin guī-tóu 'glans penis' means literally "turtle-head". (For obvious reasons, this formation is especially appropriate for the uncircumcised organ.) The data is so far insufficient to establish whether this same metaphor occurs in TB languages.(15) SEMEN <--> SHIT: A few languages associate semen with SHIT / BODY DIRT / EXCRETION. Cf. Lahu nī-qhɛ̂ "penis-shit", Chepang təyŋ-kliʔ 'id.', and Bantawa lü-khü-wa (khü 'stool, excreta'). In Jingpho ne³¹khji⁵⁵, however, this combination of morphemes means SMEGMA.(16) SEMEN <--> FAT: Cf. Lahu nī-chu 'semen' (lit. "penis-fat").Few monomorphemic etyma are reconstructible for SEMEN, with three possibilities offered below in Chapter 1.9.9: #1614, #1627, and #3486.Words for FORESKIN/PREPUCE, naturally enough, are compounds of PENIS + SKIN (see Chapter 1.6.5).Expressions for COPULATE frequently involve PENIS plus a verb. See Chapter 1.9.8.

<par>Another possible etymon (perhaps <reconstruction>*kloŋ</reconstruction>) is represented by the first syllable of Hayu <latinform>kluŋ-klaŋ</latinform> 'penis' RN#120292 and the second syllable of Written Burmese <latinform>lî-khyôŋ</latinform> 'glans penis' #118258. The latter form might be allofamically related to WB <latinform>əkhyoŋ</latinform> 'bar; long piece' #52597.</par>

<par>It should come as no surprise that verbs meaning COPULATE are closely associated with notions of LOVE and DESIRE. Most etyma in this chapter have a semantic range which encompasses both the physical act of love and its emotional concomitant. At least one root makes an overt connection between COPULATE and PENIS (cf. <xref ref="548">#548</xref>, above). Innumerable euphemisms for the act of sexual congress occur, but most of these are best treated in a separate study devoted to verbs. Typical verbs extended to a sexual sense include: PUSH (Karbi <latinform>dòy</latinform> 'push', <latinform>che-dōy</latinform> 'copulate'); DO (Jingpho <latinform>dī</latinform> 'do, make, form, fashion; be guilty of illicit intercourse'); MEET (Jingpho <latinform>khrúm</latinform> 'meet, converge; have sexual intercourse'), etc. </par>

<par>In Sino-Tibetan languages, the words for the various liquids produced by or contained in the body tend to be transparent compounds where the last element is a morpheme meaning 'water; liquid'.<footnote>Exceptions are BLOOD, URINE, SWEAT, and sometimes BILE, which are usually monosyllabic words.</footnote> Of the many body fluids,<footnote>That is, AMNIOTIC FLUID, BILE, BLOOD, MILK, PHLEGM, PUS, SALIVA, SEMEN, SNOT, SWEAT, TEARS, VAGINAL SECRETIONS.</footnote> only a few relate directly to the reproductive system (AMNIOTIC FLUID, MILK, SEMEN, VAGINAL SECRETIONS). In this chapter, however, in order to illustrate the scope of the various ST roots for WATER, I include a generous sampling of compounds referring to non-reproductive body fluids.<footnote>The numerous roots for water (over ten at last count) in the STEDT database merit a separate study. Words for WATER seldom seem to be used by themselves in ST languages to mean URINE, unlike e.g. English, where <emph>make water</emph> is a common euphemism for 'urinate'; for an exception see the WT form <latinform>c̀ʻu</latinform> below.</footnote></par>
<par>Most compounds are of the form O-V where O is “semen” and the verb is “shoot” , “wet” , or somesuch.</par>
